title: Merlin
summary: A realtime voice agent that qualifies inbound prospects in the browser, then books the briefing or captures the brief.
situation: Every inbound enquiry cost a discovery meeting before anyone knew whether it was worth having, and a missed call was a missed client. The studio was spending its scarcest asset — senior time — qualifying strangers, and still handling one caller at a time.
task: Qualify inbound prospects without putting a person on the call.
Never miss a caller, however many arrive at once.
result: Callers are handled concurrently and qualify themselves in conversation, leaving with a briefing booked or a structured brief in the team's inbox. Nothing rings out, and senior time goes to calls already worth having.
contributions: Framed the problem as qualification, not automation. The goal was never to replace discovery — it was to stop paying for discovery that shouldn't happen.
Argued the case on coverage rather than novelty: a person answers one call and works one shift. The comparison that mattered was against the role, not against a chatbot.
Split one product into two front doors once it was clear a cold prospect and a client with a spec want completely different conversations.
Ruled that the agent doesn't get the last word on whether work happened, after a live call ended announcing a brief that was never submitted.
Set the standard that a wrong booking costs more trust than a retry costs patience — so booking tools must prove they know the time before they can act.

keyResults.metricNote: SEEK's 2026 band for an Australian receptionist — the job Merlin does without, except it takes every caller at once and none out of hours.

items.name: Never rings out
items.label: Concurrent by default
items.note: A person answers one call at a time. A missed call was a missed client.

items.name: Meeting → brief
items.label: Discovery, async
items.note: A caller describes what they want built without sitting through a meeting to do it.

title: ZeroTouch Onboarding
summary: Client onboarding for an Australian accounting firm, built against a hard AML/CTF compliance deadline.
situation: Femia's team re-keyed the same client across Xero, QuickBooks and Karbon, quoted off three Excel calculators, and assembled AML evidence by hand. New AML/CTF rules turned that manual trail into a legal exposure for a $5M practice.
task: Collapse the platform-hopping into one flow, and make compliance provable before the date.
Keep pricing confidential from clients — the firm's one non-negotiable.
result: One platform instead of three. Around 20 hours a week of duplicate entry gone, and every ID and AML check now lands automatically on the client's record in the system auditors already read.
contributions: Mapped where the time was actually going before designing anything: not the work itself, but the same client being typed into Xero, then QuickBooks, then Karbon.
Found the gap that mattered for the deadline — compliance was being done, but not provably. The evidence an auditor would ask for existed nowhere a system could produce it.
Made pricing confidentiality the architectural constraint the moment the firm named it, rather than a feature bolted on later. It split the product into two forms and shaped everything after.
Decided the evidence writes into Karbon rather than a new database, so compliance lives where the firm already works and nobody learns a second system.
Caught a compliance-grade defect in testing: the practice system silently corrupts Director IDs beginning 036. Chose to preserve the identifier over using the field it belonged in.

keyResults.metricLabel: A week of duplicate work removed
keyResults.metricNote: Staff were carrying the same client between Xero, QuickBooks and Karbon by hand. One platform ended the round trip.

items.label: Platforms in the onboarding path
items.note: Client details entered once; quote, AML record and practice system stay in step on their own.

items.name: Evidence, automatically
items.label: Where auditors already look
items.note: Every ID and AML check writes to the client's timeline as it happens, not reconstructed afterwards.

title: Mofiz Marma
summary: Talk, and Mofiz draws. A voice agent that renders live Mermaid diagrams as you describe them, in English or Bangla.
situation: Describing a system takes a minute; drawing it takes an hour. Diagram tools make you stop thinking and start placing boxes, so the diagram in your head rarely survives the trip to the screen.
task: Let someone talk and watch the diagram appear.
Work in Bangla as naturally as in English.
result: Speak a system and it draws itself — flowchart, sequence, ER, mindmap — with the code editable and exportable. Idea to deployed in a single day.
contributions: Bet that the bottleneck was the drawing, not the thinking, so the product's only job is to get out of the way between the two.
Made the model fix its own diagrams — parse errors go back to it as feedback, capped — so the user never sees a broken render.
Locked the persona and tools into the session token, so the product cannot be quietly repurposed by whoever opens the page.

keyResults.metricNote: Voice, diagrams, canvas and export — the whole thing, shipped.

items.name: Talk → diagram
items.label: No boxes placed by hand
items.note: The conversation is the input; the diagram keeps up with it.

items.name: English and Bangla
items.label: Switches with the speaker
items.note: Not a language setting — it follows whoever is talking.

title: RepoMap
summary: Point it at a GitHub repo or a local folder and get architecture diagrams, module notes and a generated README.
situation: A new engineer's first week is archaeology — reading entry points, guessing at structure, rebuilding a map that already exists in someone else's head.
task: Turn a repo into a readable map: architecture, request flow, where the data goes.
Make it usable on private code, not just public repos.
result: Point it at a repo — public, or a folder on your own machine — and get three diagrams, module notes, onboarding steps and a README, cached after the first look.
contributions: Decided privacy was the adoption blocker, not accuracy. Nobody uploads their employer's codebase to a stranger's website, so private folders never leave the browser.
Chose the model on quota rather than benchmarks: a free tool that dies after 20 uses a day isn't a tool. 500 against 20 settled it.
Budgeted the prompt deliberately — a dozen ranked files beat the whole repo, cheaper and more accurate than sending everything.

keyResults.metricLabel: Daily uses the model choice bought
keyResults.metricNote: The difference between a tool people can actually try and a demo that runs out by lunchtime.

items.name: 0 uploads
items.label: Private code stays local
items.note: The folder is read in the browser, so an employer's repo never leaves the machine.

items.name: Diagrams, modules, README
items.label: From a URL or a folder
items.note: The map someone would otherwise spend their first week rebuilding by hand.

title: Git Insight
summary: Feed it a GitHub username and it returns a card-based developer assessment written for recruiters.
situation: A GitHub profile is a wall of repository names. Recruiters judge developers from it every day with no real way to read it, so good candidates get missed and bad ones get through.
task: Turn a username into a structured read: archetype, stack, strengths, role fit.
Accept whatever gets pasted — a username or a full profile URL.
result: A profile becomes a seven-card assessment written for the person doing the hiring, not the person being hired.
contributions: Wrote for the reader rather than the developer: a recruiter needs a verdict they can act on, so the model fills a fixed structure instead of producing prose someone has to interpret.
Caught before launch that the original design would have shipped my API key to every visitor — Vite inlines prefixed variables straight into the bundle. Both keys moved server-side.
Cut to the top 30 repositories: sending a whole profile costs more and says less.

keyResults.metricLabel: A wall of repo names, made readable
keyResults.metricNote: Archetype, stack, strengths, weaknesses, coding insights, projects, role fit.

items.name: Keys off the client
items.label: Caught before it shipped
items.note: Vite inlines prefixed env vars into the bundle — the first design would have leaked the key to every visitor.

items.name: Top 30, projected
items.label: Deliberate prompt budget
items.note: Ranked and cut to seven fields each, because the whole profile costs more and reads worse.

title: Socratic AI Tutor
summary: The proof of concept for ilm AI — a tutor that refuses to just answer, asking guiding questions until the student gets there themselves.
situation: Students use AI to finish assignments and learn nothing from them. ilm's bet was a tutor that refuses to answer — but that bet only pays if the refusal survives a real student pushing for the answer.
task: Prove the Socratic line holds under pressure, not just in a demo.
Show it working by voice, since that is how tutoring actually sounds.
result: A working proof of concept that held the line — guiding questions, by speech or text — and helped win ilm a $12,000 WA Government Innovation Grant.
contributions: Authored the technical proposal and requirement artifacts the grant application rested on. The proof of concept is what let ilm demonstrate the idea in the room rather than describe it.
Put the constraint in the prompt rather than hoping for it: an answer only after two or three hints, and only for general knowledge. 'Be Socratic' does not survive a student who keeps asking.
Aimed the product at the policy question, not just the classroom one. Australia's concern is children using AI safely, and a tutor that refuses to do the homework is an answer to it.
Chose the browser's own speech engines, so voice costs nothing per minute — the difference between a pilot a school can afford and one it cannot.

keyResults.metricNote: Secured within three months, on the technical proposal and requirement artifacts I authored.

items.name: Safe AI for children
items.label: Aimed at the policy, not just the market
items.note: A tutor that refuses to do the homework answers the question Australian regulators are actually asking.

items.name: $0 per voice minute
items.label: Speech runs in the browser
items.note: No audio vendor, no per-seat cost — the pricing question a school asks first.

title: Otsukare
summary: A Japan-focused freelance platform connecting global IT talent with Japanese firms through AI-driven matching.
situation: METI projects Japan will be short 400,000 IT workers. The talent exists globally — the barrier is that Japanese firms and foreign engineers cannot find or assess one another across the language and process gap.
task: Connect global IT talent to Japanese firms with matching that survives the language gap.
Win the prefecture stakeholders who would have to back it.
result: A platform argued against METI's own shortfall figure, and acknowledged by the Vice Chairman of Miyazaki Prefecture — the project's key stakeholder.
contributions: Grounded the thesis in METI's own gap analysis rather than a market guess. 400,000 is the government's number, which made it the room's number too — and made the problem theirs rather than ours to prove.
Took the case to the prefecture directly rather than to the market first. In Japan, the backing that mattered came from the government side.
Positioned AI matching as the bridge across the assessment gap, not as the product. The barrier was never the supply of engineers — it was firms being unable to judge them.

keyResults.metricNote: Japan's own ministry figure — the gap the platform exists to close, and the number that opened the conversation.

items.name: Vice Chairman, Miyazaki Prefecture
items.label: Key stakeholder — acknowledged
items.note: The endorsement that mattered came from the government side rather than the market side.

title: Fabric QR Web App
summary: Shares fabric details — images, GSM, type, references — over QR codes, killing a repetitive workflow and its errors.
situation: Every time anyone asked about a fabric, someone photographed it, typed out the GSM, type and references, and sent it on WhatsApp. Same fabric, same details, again for every person who asked — and a typo travelled exactly as far as the photo did.
task: Kill the repetition: capture a fabric once, share it any number of times.
Make the spec travel with the photo so nothing gets retyped.
result: Photograph the fabric, get a QR, share the QR. Five minutes of photographing and retyping became ten seconds.
contributions: Traced the cost to repetition rather than effort. The task was never hard — it was done again from scratch for every person who asked, which is why nobody had flagged it as a problem.
Made the QR the deliverable instead of the message. One code carries the image, GSM, type and references, so the spec stops being retyped and stops drifting between people.
Fixed the workflow where it already lived. People were going to keep working over WhatsApp, so the output had to be one shareable thing — not another system to log into.

keyResults.metricNote: Thirty times faster, and the details stop depending on who typed them that time.

items.name: Typed once, not every time
items.label: So the errors go too
items.note: A retyped spec is a spec that can drift. The QR carries the original.

title: DevMentor
summary: An AI mock interviewer for fresh CS graduates — a real technical interview by voice, then a scored report on how it went.
situation: Fresh graduates fail interviews they could pass, because the only place to practise is a real one. There is nowhere low-stakes to be bad first, and the feedback from a rejection is silence.
task: Give a graduate a realistic technical interview they can fail privately.
Send them away knowing what to fix, not just how it felt.
result: Reached 500+ active users within 14 days. A voice interview with a live transcript, then a scored report — strengths, gaps, and a breakdown by skill.
contributions: Made users bring their own API key. That one decision is why 500 people could arrive in a fortnight without an inference bill — the product scales at zero marginal cost, and I never hold anyone's credentials.
Insisted the output be a verdict, not a transcript. A chat log tells a graduate nothing; a 0-100 score with strengths, gaps and per-skill feedback tells them what to practise on Monday.
Chose voice over text, because what graduates actually fail is speaking under pressure — not writing answers with time to think.
Watched onboarding and iterated on the feature set against real drop-off rather than opinion.

keyResults.metricNote: Reached with no marketing spend and no inference bill — the cost model is why the growth was affordable.

items.name: $0 marginal cost
items.label: Bring your own key
items.note: Every user pays for their own inference, so a thousand more costs the same as one.

items.name: A score, not a chat log
items.label: 0-100, with a breakdown
items.note: Technical knowledge, communication and problem solving — each rated, each with what to fix.

title: Siratul Mustakim
summary: A voice-first field app that tells a preacher which house to visit next, draws the route, and records the outcome.
situation: Door-to-door outreach forgets. Who was visited, who actually answered, who is overdue — it lives in volunteers' heads, and someone walking a street cannot stop to update a spreadsheet.
task: Make the record keep itself, hands-free, while a volunteer walks.
Send people to the door that most needs them, not the one that happens to be nearest.
result: A volunteer is told where to go, shown the route, and records what happened by speaking. Piloted with Masjidul Ibrahim — the team's memory survives the walk instead of ending with it.
contributions: Found the distinction the whole model turns on: a knock is not contact. Only an answered door resets the clock, so the map reflects conversations rather than attempts.
Ruled the agent may never invent navigation. A confidently wrong street name sends a volunteer to a stranger's door — worse than saying nothing at all.
Built it as an agent rather than a form: the volunteer talks, and the tools behind the conversation pick the house, draw the route and file the outcome.
Cut the server entirely once it was clear the browser could hold the conversation. A community organisation shouldn't carry hosting costs between walks.

keyResults.metricNote: Built for CIEA and put in front of a real congregation's outreach team, rather than left as a demo.

items.name: Hands-free
items.label: The whole loop, while walking
items.note: Which house, what route, what happened — spoken, with the phone still in a pocket.

items.name: 14 / 42 days
items.label: Warm and cooling thresholds
items.note: Priority follows when someone last answered, so nobody is forgotten or knocked twice.

title: Narawe
summary: Document intelligence for professional-services firms — scan client PDFs and search them by word or by meaning.
situation: Professional-services firms hold years of client history in scanned paper. Finding one document meant people searching by hand for hours — so firms staffed for the searching rather than the work.
task: Get paper in with no friction, including straight from a phone camera.
Make it findable by the words people half-remember and the ideas they can't name.
result: A firm's scanned history becomes searchable by phrase and by meaning. Hours of hunting collapse into a query, and the people left are verifying results rather than looking for them.
contributions: Reframed the cost from time to headcount: firms weren't only losing hours, they were staffing around a search problem. That reframing is what made the business case.
Identified capture as the real adoption risk, not accuracy. A system nobody feeds is a system nobody uses, so scanning had to work from the phone already in someone's hand.
Chose to fuse keyword and semantic search rather than pick one: the exact phrase on an invoice and the vague idea of a letter are different questions, and firms ask both.
Consolidated three AI vendors into one — fewer contracts, fewer failure modes, one bill to defend.
Made the AI summary fire-and-forget. An assistant that can fail someone's upload is worse than no assistant.

keyResults.metricNote: What is left is verifying what came back, not hunting for it — hours of manual searching become a query.

items.name: Phrase and meaning
items.label: One search, both questions
items.note: Fused results with matched words highlighted, so people trust what came back.

items.label: AI vendors consolidated
items.note: Clarifai, OpenRouter and OpenAI became one — two supplier relationships and three failure modes removed.
